tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - VMOVHPD: 4 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
hint.details:
- Constant non-unit stride: 10 occurrence(s)
Non-unit stride (uncontiguous) accesses are not efficiently using data caches
hint.title: Slow data structures access
hint.txt: Detected data structures (typically arrays) that cannot be efficiently read/written

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 6 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 48 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 48: addition or subtraction
The binary loop is loading 1024 bytes (128 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 128 bytes (16 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.04 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

hint.workaround: Unroll your loop if trip count is significantly higher than target unroll factor and if some data references are common to consecutive iterations. This can be done manually. Or by recompiling with -funroll-loops and/or -floop-unroll-and-jam. Or with the unroll (resp. unroll_and_jam) directive on top of the inner (resp. surrounding) loop. You can enforce an unroll factor: !GCC$ unroll N
hint.title: Unroll opportunity
hint.txt: Loop is potentially data access bound.

 - FP4 (256 bits): FP store data
 - FP5 (256 bits): FP store data

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 16.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 66% of peak load performance is reached (64.00 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 12% of peak store performance is reached (8.00 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header: 12% of peak computational performance is used (3.00 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)<<image_col_maj>>
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
66% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 0% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 58% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 16.00 to 12.50 cycles (1.28x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Reduce the number of FP add instructions
 - Read less array elements
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by:
 - execution of FP add operations (the FP add unit is a bottleneck)
 - reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
 - writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 16.00 to 10.50 cycles (1.52x speedup).
